title: Anatomy of a cold start
description: A cold start adds instance provisioning, runtime bootstrap, and init code before the first invocation; warm instances skip all of it.
body: Use when explaining tail latency. A cold start occurs when no warm instance is available: the platform provisions an instance, downloads and starts the runtime and code package, and runs init (module-scope code) before handling the request; warm invocations reuse a ready instance and skip this. Larger packages, heavy dependency graphs, and slow-starting runtimes lengthen it. Do not confuse cold-start latency with handler latency. Actions: measure init separately and reduce package/init cost. Validation signals include distinct init vs invocation durations in traces. Risk boundary: optimizing handler code when the spike is init time wastes effort — attribute the latency correctly first.
